series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/3] cxl/core/region: move pmem region driver logic into region_pmem.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the logic for managing persistent memory (pmem) regions from the overloaded region.c file into a new, dedicated region_pmem.c file. This separation makes it clear that this code only applies to pmem regions and helps to declutter the original file. No functional changes are made as part of this refactoring effort.

review_comments.author: Alison Schofield
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch always adds region_pmem.o regardless of CONFIG_CXL_PMEM, and questioned whether the intention is to conditionally compile it based on this configuration option.
review_comments.raw_body:
This always adds region_pmem.o regardless of CONFIG_CXL_PMEM.
Are you heading towards conditionally compiling, where region_pmem.o is
omitted if !CONFIG_CXL_PMEM? 

Similar question for region_dax.o

--Alison

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges reviewer's concern about adding more Kconfig cruft, but doesn't commit to a fix; instead suggests that the current approach might be acceptable despite potential bloat for some deployments.
review_comments.raw_body:
I hadn't considered going that far, but it doesn't seem unreasonable?

It just seemed natural to break out the code this way, but I don't know
if adding more Kconfig cruft is worth it just to save some .o's and a
smaller amount of code.

The recent pmem/nvdimm Kconfig change does kind of show that we might
have some bloat for some deployments which might not care about pmem -
for example.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Jonathan Cameron
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the cxl_pmem_region_release function is missing from region_pmem.c and requested it be added to properly release pmem regions

Reviewer Jonathan Cameron suggested that having region.o and region_pmem.o on one line in the Makefile makes it clearer that they are either both included or neither is, but noted that this would require more changes if the CONFIG_* definitions were later split up.

Reviewer noted that the lifetime management of cxl_pmem_region is more complex than desired, but did not provide specific technical details or suggestions for improvement.

Reviewer Jonathan Cameron noted that removing a blank line from the code could be unnecessary, as there was already one present in the original file and questioned its removal.

The reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a pmem region is deleted, and requested that the code be updated to properly clean up resources when a pmem region is removed.

Reviewer Jonathan Cameron expressed concern that the lifetime management of cxl_pmem_region is overly complex, but noted it's not a problem for this specific patch and couldn't immediately suggest improvements.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 6/6] mm/memcontrol: Make memory.high tier-aware
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Proposed patch to make memory.high tier-aware, but raised concerns about incorrect heuristics and potential OOM issues.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified incorrect heuristics in the proposed patch that could drive swap usage and invert LRU. Suggested revising the logic to manage top-tier memory for noisy-neighbor issues.

review_comments.author: Donet Tom
review_comments.summary: Expressed concern that demotion to lower tier under cgroup memory pressure could still trigger OOM, and asked if this is an issue to address.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/24/26 8:53 PM, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 04:21:06PM +0530, Donet Tom wrote:
>> IIUC The intent of this patch is to partition cgroup memory such that
>> 0 \u2192 toptier_high is backed by higher-tier memory, and
>> toptier_high \u2192 max is backed by lower-tier memory.
>>
>> Based on this:
>>
>> 1.If top-tier usage exceeds toptier_high, pages should be
>>   demoted to the lower tier.
>>
>> 2. If lower-tier usage exceeds (max - toptier_high), pages
>>   should be swapped out.
>>
> This is not accurate and an incorrect heuristic.
>
> Transiently, lower-tier usage may exceed (max - toptier_high) for any
> number of reasons which should not be used as signal for pushing swap.
>
> driving swap usage is a function of (usage > memory.high) without regard
> for toptier / lowtier.
>
>> 3. If total memory usage exceeds max, demotion should be
>>   avoided and reclaim should directly swap out pages.
>>
> This is also incorrect, as it would drive agingin inversions.
> Demotion is a natural extension of the LRU infrastructure:
>
> toptier active -> toptier inactive -> lowtier inactive -> swap
>
> if you do (toptier inactive -> swap) you have inverted the LRU.


Thanks, Gregory, for the clarification.

One remaining concern is that under cgroup memory pressure,
demotion to the lower tier can still happen. Since demotion
does not uncharge the memcg, this could still trigger OOM.

Is this an issue we should address?


>
> As far as I know, from testing, we retain all the existing behavior - we
> are just managing a limited resource (top tier memory) to manage the
> noisy-neighbor issue.  So...
>
>
>> Should we also handle cases (2) and (3) in this patch?
> No, I don't think we should
>
> ~Gregory
>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] nfs: fix utimensat() for atime with delegated timestamps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS client where delegated timestamps don't properly update the ctime when only the atime is changed using utimensat(). The problem occurs because there's no mtime update to trigger the delegated attribute update. To fix this, the client now issues an RPC to update the ctime alongside the atime.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] nfs: update inode ctime after removexattr operation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the inode ctime after a removexattr operation in NFSv4.2 to ensure that the cached timestamp is refreshed. The problem it solves is that the stat() system call does not trigger a GETATTR request when delegated timestamps are used, resulting in an outdated ctime value. To fix this, the patch adds a trailing GETATTR request to the removexattr operation, similar to how setxattr operations update the inode's timestamp. This change ensures that the inode's ctime is accurately updated after removing extended attributes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues in the NFS delegated attribute handling, specifically fixing problems with utimensat() and removexattr operations on NFSv4.2 file systems. The patches update inode timestamps and ctime accordingly to ensure consistency and correctness.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/9] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching and sending the next request in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation from the io_uring interface. The duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() is removed, decoupling request fetching from the send operation. This simplifies the control flow and reduces unnecessary parameter passing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/9] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the io-uring header copying logic in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver by moving it into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(). This makes the code more clear and centralized, allowing for better error handling and rate-limited logging. The function takes three arguments: the user-space ring buffer address, the header to be copied, and its size. It uses the copy_to_user() function to perform the actual copying and returns an error if it fails. The patch modifies several functions in the dev_uring.c file to use this new function instead of duplicating the copying logic.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 3/9] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module to make it more clear and centralized. It introduces a new function, copy_header_from_ring(), which handles the actual copying of data from user space to kernel space. This change improves error handling and rate-limited logging. The existing code is updated to use this new function instead of directly calling copy_from_user().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 4/9] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This simplifies both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying, particularly when kernel-managed buffer rings are added. The patch adds a new enum type `fuse_uring_header_type` with values for different header types, and modifies two inline functions `copy_header_to_ring` and `copy_header_from_ring` to use this enum type. This change will make the code more maintainable and easier to understand.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 5/9] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver, specifically for payload copying. A new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state()` is introduced to encapsulate this logic, making it more modular and easier to maintain. The existing code is simplified by removing duplicated functionality and replacing it with calls to this new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 6/9] fuse: support buffer copying for kernel addresses
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer copying in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module, specifically allowing it to work directly on kernel addresses rather than requiring page extraction and kmapping. This is a preparatory step for supporting kernel-managed ring buffers in fuse-over-io-uring. The change involves modifying several functions within the FUSE codebase to handle both user-space and kernel-space buffer copying. The patch also includes some minor cleanups and warnings to ensure correct behavior when working with kernel addresses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 7/9] fuse: add io-uring kernel-managed buffer ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for a kernel-managed buffer ring to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring interface. The buffer ring allows for more efficient communication between the server and the kernel by eliminating the need for pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses, and also reduces memory usage per queue. The feature is enabled on a per-queue basis and requires preregistration of a kernel-managed buffer ring and a memory region to hold the headers. This patch has two benefits: it eliminates overhead in server-kernel interactions and reduces memory needed for buffers per queue.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 8/9] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zero-copy data transfer over io-uring for the FUSE file system. It allows privileged servers to preregister a kernel-managed buffer ring, a registered memory region, and a sparse buffer corresponding to the queue depth. This enables the elimination of memory copies between the kernel and userspace for read/write operations, resulting in improved performance. Benchmarks show approximately 20-25% increases in throughput for certain workloads. The patch modifies several files in the FUSE codebase to implement this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 9/9] docs: fuse: add io-uring bufring and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy techniques. The new documentation explains how to set up kernel-managed buffer rings, which reduce memory allocation requirements and eliminate overheads associated with user page pinning and translation. It also describes the high-level process of using these buffer rings in FUSE operations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer ring and zero-copy capabilities to the FUSE file system over io-uring, reducing memory overhead and enabling direct kernel-to-userspace operations. The changes eliminate the need for pinning and unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses, allowing for incremental buffer consumption and non-overlapping regions of a buffer to be used simultaneously across multiple requests. Zero-copy functionality is only available for privileged servers and allows the server to directly operate on the client's underlying pages, resulting in approximately 20-25% increase in throughput for read-heavy operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename io_buffer_register_bvec()/io_buffer_unregister_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames two io_uring APIs: `io_buffer_register_bvec()` and `io_buffer_unregister_bvec()`. The first is renamed to `io_buffer_register_request()` to prepare for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, which will register bvecs directly. The second is renamed to `io_buffer_unregister()` to make its name more generic, as it will be used by both the new and old APIs for unregistration. This change is part of a larger effort to support kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/5] io_uring/rsrc: split io_buffer_register_request() logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits the main initialization logic in io_buffer_register_request() into a helper function called io_kernel_buffer_init(). This is done to prepare for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, which will reuse this logic. The new function initializes an io_mapped_ubuf structure and allocates memory for it. It also sets up various fields in the structure, including the buffer length, direction, and release function. The original function now calls the helper function to perform the initialization, making the code more modular and easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_buffer_register_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, io_buffer_register_bvec(), which allows registering an array of bio vectors (bvecs) to the io_uring context. The function is designed for use in fuse-over-io-uring zero-copy operations and provides a way to register multiple bvecs at once. It takes several parameters, including the io_uring command, the bvec array, and flags for issue and release. The patch also adds an inline function for the case where the feature is not supported. The new function is implemented in the io_uring/rsrc.c file and includes error handling and locking to ensure thread safety.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename and export IO_IMU_DEST / IO_IMU_SOURCE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames and exports two io_uring flags, IO_IMU_DEST and IO_IMU_SOURCE, to more descriptive names, IO_BUF_DEST and IO_BUF_SOURCE. The rename is necessary because callers may need the buffer to be both readable and writable. The flags are exported so that other subsystems can use them. The patch updates several files, including io_uring_types.h, io_uring.c, rsrc.c, and rsrc.h, making changes such as adding new enum values, updating BUILD_BUG_ON macros, and modifying code to use the new flag names.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_uring_registered_mem_region_get()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function `io_uring_registered_mem_region_get()` to the io_uring command handlers. The function allows for retrieving the vmapped pointer to the ring's registered memory region and its size, enabling direct access to pre-registered memory for passing data between the kernel and server. This is specifically used by fuse for reading/writing header data. The patch introduces a new function in `io_uring/rsrc.c` and updates the corresponding inline functions in `include/linux/io_uring/cmd.h`. The function takes three parameters: an io_uring command, a pointer to store the number of pages, and issue flags.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series refactors and extends the io_uring registered buffers infrastructure to allow external subsystems to register pre-existing bvec arrays directly and obtain a pointer to the registered memory region, making fuse zero-copy possible.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v3 0/5] io_uring: extend bvec registration and add mem region lookup
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch renames two io_uring APIs: `io_buffer_register_bvec()` and `io_buffer_unregister_bvec()`. The first is renamed to `io_buffer_register_request()` to prepare for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, which will register bvecs directly. The second is renamed to `io_buffer_unregister()` to make its name more generic, as it will be used by both the new and old APIs for unregistration. This change is part of a larger effort to support kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/7] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues in the list_lru code, specifically in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function. The problem is that this function can return NULL when !skip_empty, which leads to incorrect behavior. The patch removes these NULL checks and ensures that the function always acquires a lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is a specific use case that requires this feature. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, `folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc`, which allocates list_lru heads for folio objects under the control of memory cgroups (memcgs). The function is designed to be lightweight and RCU-safe, allowing it to quickly check if the list_lru heads already exist for a given memcg. If they do not exist, it allocates them. This patch is part of a larger effort to switch the THP shrinker to use list_lru, which requires this helper function to manage folio objects correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation is reclaiming memory. The change also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is consistent with how other major shrinkers use list_lru.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that the deferred split shrinker's list_lru allocation is not properly synchronized, and suggested making the code more concise.

The reviewer pointed out that the patch's explanation of the deferred split shrinker's call flow is unclear and suggested a more accurate representation by tracing through the function calls from shrink_slab_memcg() to deferred_split_scan().

The reviewer noted that the deferred split queue uses a per-memcg list, but also sets a bit in shrinker_info->unit->map, which is used to determine the shrink_control in shrink_slab_memcg(), potentially leading to confusion or incorrect behavior.

The reviewer noted that the deferred_split_scan() function walks the same memcg->split_queue regardless of node, which they consider suboptimal.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested exposing deferred_split_lru as a getter or creating a new function folio_memcg_alloc_deferred to encapsulate the allocation of the list lru, allowing callers outside huge_memory.c to access it without having to use the global variable.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested splitting the huge zero folio handling into its own function, but noted it's out of scope for this series and should be addressed in a future patch.

The reviewer questioned whether there's a potential race condition when the shrinker is freed, causing the deferred_split_lru to be accessed by another thread.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested renaming the 'lru' variable to something more descriptive, citing that single-letter variable names are not conventional

Reviewer Lorenzo questioned why the deferred split queue was switched to list_lru for anonymous pages specifically, indicating a lack of understanding of the change and requesting clarification.

Reviewer Lorenzo expressed concern about a hidden RCU lock in the code and suggested refactoring the function, possibly splitting out folio_ref_freeze() bits into a separate helper function.

Reviewer Lorenzo pointed out a minor issue with variable naming conventions, specifically that single-letter variable names can be confusing and suggested that 'l' is particularly problematic due to its similarity in appearance to the digit 1.

The reviewer suggested refactoring the code to avoid duplicated logic in list_lru_del(), specifically by making it reference _deferred_list explicitly, instead of having it hardcoded in multiple places.

The reviewer questioned the need for holding a lock while calling list_lru_add(), suggesting that using a locked_add_variant would be more intuitive and avoiding the need for irqsave/restore.

Reviewer Lorenzo agrees that the partially mapped logic is correct, as folio_try_get() will only fail to add a reference if the folio has already lost the race.

The reviewer suggests adding an irqsave/restore variant of list_lru_add() to handle the repeating pattern of acquiring and releasing locks in interrupt-safe manner.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested moving a stat counter increment operation underneath an if statement to prevent it from being incremented when the operation fails.

The reviewer suggests refactoring the large if-branch in deferred_split_folio() into a separate patch for better bisectability and reviewability.

The reviewer expressed appreciation for the patch's code deletion and thanked the submitter, indicating a positive sentiment.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> 	alloc/unmap
> 	  deferred_split_folio()
> 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 	          deferred_split_scan()
> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

MEMORY MANAGEMENT - THP (TRANSPARENT HUGE PAGE)

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 6/6] mm/memcontrol: Make memory.high tier-aware
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Proposed patch to make memory.high tier-aware, but raised concerns about incorrect heuristics and potential OOM issues.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified incorrect heuristics in the proposed patch that could drive swap usage and invert LRU. Suggested revising the logic to manage top-tier memory for noisy-neighbor issues.

review_comments.author: Donet Tom
review_comments.summary: Expressed concern that demotion to lower tier under cgroup memory pressure could still trigger OOM, and asked if this is an issue to address.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/24/26 8:53 PM, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 04:21:06PM +0530, Donet Tom wrote:
>> IIUC The intent of this patch is to partition cgroup memory such that
>> 0 \u2192 toptier_high is backed by higher-tier memory, and
>> toptier_high \u2192 max is backed by lower-tier memory.
>>
>> Based on this:
>>
>> 1.If top-tier usage exceeds toptier_high, pages should be
>>   demoted to the lower tier.
>>
>> 2. If lower-tier usage exceeds (max - toptier_high), pages
>>   should be swapped out.
>>
> This is not accurate and an incorrect heuristic.
>
> Transiently, lower-tier usage may exceed (max - toptier_high) for any
> number of reasons which should not be used as signal for pushing swap.
>
> driving swap usage is a function of (usage > memory.high) without regard
> for toptier / lowtier.
>
>> 3. If total memory usage exceeds max, demotion should be
>>   avoided and reclaim should directly swap out pages.
>>
> This is also incorrect, as it would drive agingin inversions.
> Demotion is a natural extension of the LRU infrastructure:
>
> toptier active -> toptier inactive -> lowtier inactive -> swap
>
> if you do (toptier inactive -> swap) you have inverted the LRU.


Thanks, Gregory, for the clarification.

One remaining concern is that under cgroup memory pressure,
demotion to the lower tier can still happen. Since demotion
does not uncharge the memcg, this could still trigger OOM.

Is this an issue we should address?


>
> As far as I know, from testing, we retain all the existing behavior - we
> are just managing a limited resource (top tier memory) to manage the
> noisy-neighbor issue.  So...
>
>
>> Should we also handle cases (2) and (3) in this patch?
> No, I don't think we should
>
> ~Gregory
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] generic/747: handle ENOSPC gracefully during write/delete cycles

version_history.subject: [PATCH] generic/747: handle ENOSPC gracefully during write/delete cycles

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a test failure issue in the generic/747 test, which consistently fails on btrfs due to ENOSPC errors during write/delete cycles. The problem arises from statfs overestimating available space at high fill levels, causing dd to hit ENOSPC even when there's room. To handle this, the patch breaks out of the fill loop when full, deletes a file to free space and retry on failure, and redirects dd stderr to preserve errors for debugging purposes. The test is designed to stress garbage collection through write/delete churn, not to validate space accounting.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that the patch's ENOSPC handling may cause unexpected issues due to btrfs' statfs implementation overestimating available space, and suggested addressing this underlying filesystem bug for the benefit of users.
review_comments.raw_body:
f_bavail overestimating is a btrfs implementation bug that needs fixing.
It can be wrong, but it needs to be too low in doubt.  Without this
you're going to cause unexpected ENOSPC for real life applictions as
well (and btrfs does have a bit of a reputation for that, so fixing
this properly will benefit your users!)

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that the issue of ENOSPC during write/delete cycles could be addressed by improving the calculation of f_bavail, rather than handling it in the test code.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that the issue in patch generic/747 is caused by overestimation of available space due to two factors: btrfs_calc_available_data_space() not handling uneven disk spaces and total_free_data calculation not considering reserved space. He recommended trying a fix for this problem.
review_comments.raw_body:
If this is the problem, then I believe we should fix our f_bavail 
calculation.

---

From what I can see, there are several different ways that f_bavail is 
overestimated:

- btrfs_calc_available_data_space()
   That functions goes through each device and try to find unallocated
   space.
   This can not handle uneven space (e.g. 1G + 10G RAID0), and it should
   go through the  per-profile estimation, which handles uneven disks way
   better.

   Normally it shouldn't affect single device, but we can still over-
   estimate by taking the reserved 1M bytes as available.

- total_free_data calculation
   It's just on-disk size minus the on-disk used space, which doesn't
   take things like reserved space into consideration.

   Thus I guess the problem is mostly from this part.

Mind to give this idea a try to fix the long failing g747?

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the generic/301 test to skip extent count checks on btrfs file systems, as the current threshold was designed for XFS and is not applicable to btrfs due to its different metadata reservation behavior.

\u5728 2026/3/25 07:10, Leo Martins \u5199\u9053:
> The 2/3 fragmentation threshold was designed for XFS's cowextsize
> mechanism and is not applicable to btrfs. After commit 0dc118b3c327
> ("btrfs: be less aggressive with metadata overcommit when we can do
> full flushing"), btrfs commits transactions more frequently during
> writeback, reducing the extent allocator's opportunity to coalesce
> adjacent CoW extents. This causes the extent count to sometimes cross
> the threshold, making the test flaky on btrfs.
> 
> Skip only the extent count assertion for btrfs while keeping the CoW
> write + remount + md5sum data integrity checks, which remain useful.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: fix stale errno check in btrfs_encoded_read/write helpers

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: fix stale errno check in btrfs_encoded_read/write helpers

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the btrfs_encoded_read and btrfs_encoded_write helpers, which incorrectly check for an error condition due to a stale errno value. The issue arises when library initialization code sets errno before the atoll() function is called, causing the helper to print usage and exit unnecessarily. To fix this, the patch clears errno before each atoll() call, ensuring that the correct error handling occurs.

> > The test fills a filesystem to 95% then does mixed write/delete cycles,
> > using statfs to decide whether to write or delete. However, statfs
> > f_bavail may overestimate the actual available space. On btrfs, the
> > statfs implementation documents its estimate as "a close approximation"
> > (fs/btrfs/super.c). At high fill levels the discrepancy between what
> > statfs reports and what the filesystem can actually allocate becomes
> > significant, causing dd to hit ENOSPC even though statfs indicated
> > there was room.
> > This is not a filesystem bug. The filesystem correctly rejects the
> > write when it cannot reserve space. The test's purpose is to stress
> > garbage collection through write/delete churn, not to validate space
> > accounting.

f_bavail overestimating is a btrfs implementation bug that needs fixing.
It can be wrong, but it needs to be too low in doubt.  Without this
you're going to cause unexpected ENOSPC for real life applictions as
well (and btrfs does have a bit of a reputation for that, so fixing
this properly will benefit your users!)

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 00/21] Virtual Swap Space
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces virtual swap space, a new approach to managing swap memory. It addresses lock contention issues on the virtual swap layer and reduces memory overhead by optimizing the swap descriptor size. The patches also fix various regressions reported by kernel developers and users, including deadlocks and poor swap free batching behavior.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the swap cache needs to be decoupled from physical swap infrastructure and will manage it at the virtual swap layer. They plan to remove swap cache related helpers of swap table, repurpose the rest of the swap table as rmap (physical -> virtual swap mapping), and temporarily use a single global lock for swap cache accesses.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the organization of the swap API in include/linux/swap.h by grouping functions into categories and removing extern keywords, stating that this is purely a clean-up with no functional changes intended.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2, and provided a generic API abstraction for this behavior.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed a concern about the lack of abstraction in zswap entry operations by adding new helper functions to facilitate re-implementation when swap is virtualized.

Author addressed a concern about the swap_zswap_tree() function being called without checking if it's empty, acknowledged that this is an oversight and agreed to replace these calls with zswap_entry_load() or zswap_entry_erase() in v2. The author also removed unnecessary code and improved error handling.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the swapcached state being managed at the virtual swap layer by abstracting away the function in userfaultfd.c to check if a swap entry is cached, and provided patches to implement this abstraction.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the patch series being incomplete, specifically that it doesn't include any behavioral changes yet. The author explains that they are only adding scaffolding for future code and that no behavioral change is intended at this time.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Kairui Song reported significant regressions in both sequential and concurrent swap workloads after testing v5 of the patch series, including a major performance regression even with a simple setup using memhog. The reviewer provided detailed test results showing slowdowns of up to 230% compared to the original version.

Reviewer Kairui Song expressed concerns about the virtual swap space implementation, citing increased overhead and potential performance issues due to the introduction of an intermediate XArray layer. They also worry that this approach may undo recent efforts to reduce memory usage and per-entry overhead. Additionally, they mentioned the need for careful consideration of swapoff support and suggested exploring a runtime-optional virtual layer.
review_comments.raw_body:
I tested the v5 (including the batched-free hotfix) and am still
seeing significant regressions in both sequential and concurrent swap
workloads

Thanks for the update as I can see It's a lot of thoughtful work.
Actually I did run some tests already with your previously posted
hotfix based on v3. I didn't update the result because very
unfortunately, I still see a major performance regression even with a
very simple setup.

BTW there seems a simpler way to reproduce that, just use memhog:
sudo mkswap /dev/pmem0; sudo swapon /dev/pmem0; time memhog 48G; sudo swapoff -a

Before:
(I'm using fish shell on that test machine so this is fish time format):
________________________________________________________
Executed in   20.80 secs    fish           external
   usr time    5.14 secs    0.00 millis    5.14 secs
   sys time   15.65 secs    1.17 millis   15.65 secs
________________________________________________________
Executed in   21.69 secs    fish           external
   usr time    5.31 secs  725.00 micros    5.31 secs
   sys time   16.36 secs  579.00 micros   16.36 secs
________________________________________________________
Executed in   21.86 secs    fish           external
   usr time    5.39 secs    1.02 millis    5.39 secs
   sys time   16.46 secs    0.27 millis   16.46 secs

After:
________________________________________________________
Executed in   30.77 secs    fish           external
   usr time    5.16 secs  767.00 micros    5.16 secs
   sys time   25.59 secs  580.00 micros   25.59 secs
________________________________________________________
Executed in   37.47 secs    fish           external
   usr time    5.48 secs    0.00 micros    5.48 secs
   sys time   31.98 secs  674.00 micros   31.98 secs
________________________________________________________
Executed in   31.34 secs    fish           external
   usr time    5.22 secs    0.00 millis    5.22 secs
   sys time   26.09 secs    1.30 millis   26.09 secs

It's obviously a lot slower.

pmem may seem rare but SSDs are good at sequential, and memhog uses
the same filled page and backend like ZRAM has extremely low overhead
for same filled pages. Results with ZRAM are very similar, and many
production workloads have massive amounts of samefill memory.

For example on the Android phone I'm using right now at this moment:
# cat /sys/block/zram0/mm_stat
4283899904 1317373036 1370259456        0 1475977216   116457  1991851
   87273  1793760
~450M of samefill page in ZRAM, we may see more on some server
workload. And I'm seeing similar memhog results with ZRAM, pmem is
just easier to setup and less noisy. also simulates high speed
storage.

I also ran the previous usemem matrix, which seems better than V3 but
still pretty bad:
Test: usemem --init-time -O -n 1 56G, 16G mem, 48G swap, avgs of 8 run.
Before:
Throughput (Sum): 528.98 MB/s Throughput (Mean): 526.113333 MB/s Free
Latency: 3037932.888889
After:
Throughput (Sum): 453.74 MB/s Throughput (Mean): 454.875000 MB/s Free
Latency: 5001144.500000 (~10%, 64% slower)

I'm not sure why our results differ so much — perhaps different LRU
settings, memory pressure ratios, or THP/mTHP configs? Here's my exact
config in the attachment. Also includes the full log and info, with
all debug options disabled for close to production. I ran it 8 times
and just attached the first result log, it's all similar anyway, my
test framework reboot the machine after each test run to reduce any
potential noise.

And the above tests are only about sequential performance, concurrent
ones seem worse:
Test: usemem --init-time -O -R -n 32 622M, 16G mem, 48G swap, avgs of 8 run.
Before:
Throughput (Sum): 5467.51 MB/s Throughput (Mean): 170.04 MB/s Free
Latency: 28648.65
After:
Throughput (Sum): 4914.86 MB/s Throughput (Mean): 152.74 MB/s Free
Latency: 67789.81 (~10%, 230% slower)

And I double checked I'm testing your latest V5 commit here:
commit 9114ebedb82089ebd3519854964c73d3959b10c0 (HEAD -> upstream/vswap)
Author: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Mar 20 12:27:35 2026 -0700

    vswap: batch contiguous vswap free calls

    In vswap_free(), we release and reacquire the cluster lock for every
    single entry, even for non-disk-swap backends where the lock drop is
    unnecessary. Batch consecutive free operations to avoid this overhead.

    Signed-off-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>

The two kernels being tested:
/boot/vmlinuz-6.19.0.orig-g05f7e89ab973
/boot/vmlinuz-6.19.0.ptch-g9114ebedb820



These tests above are done with an EPYC 7K62, I also setup an Intel
8255C with fresh installed upstream Fedora, and using Fedora's kernel
config. So far the result matches, the gap seems smaller but still

---

3 test run on 8255C using fresh installed Fedora and Fedora kernel config:
taskset -c 3 /usr/local/bin/usemem --init-time -O -n 1 112G
(That's a two nodes large machine so I pin the thread on CPU 3 for stability)

Before:
135291469824 bytes / 124326887 usecs = 1062687 KB/s
2157355 usecs to free memory
135291469824 bytes / 123930024 usecs = 1066090 KB/s
2244083 usecs to free memory
135291469824 bytes / 123484528 usecs = 1069936 KB/s
2268364 usecs to free memory

After:
135291469824 bytes / 127073712 usecs = 1039716 KB/s
3050394 usecs to free memory
135291469824 bytes / 130724757 usecs = 1010677 KB/s
3064270 usecs to free memory
135291469824 bytes / 127248347 usecs = 1038289 KB/s
3035986 usecs to free memory

And beside these known cases, my main concern is still that a
mandatory virtual layer seems just wrong, it changes how swap work in
many ways. Storage folks have been trying to bypass the kernel for
decades, as abstraction layers come with overhead — that's common
knowledge. Swap lives right at the intersection of storage and mm and
has to stay inside the kernel, so we really want the kernel path to be
as flat and direct as possible.

I'm also worried this risks undoing all the recent and upcoming work
for reducing memory usage and performance. We've been trying to shrink
per-entry overhead (I'm already feeling nervous over the current
8-byte per-entry cost, and hope soon we'll get down to <1–3 bytes).
The series mentions 24 bytes of overhead, but when I account for the
reverse mapping, it looks >32 bytes per entry.

The intermediate large XArray layer also worries me as the swap space
is now very large. The virtual size could grow with no limit. e.g. a 1
TB swap space would be a 4 layers radix tree, increasing global
contention (int(1024 * 1024 / 2) >> 6 >> 6 >> 6 == 2) and vswap could
be even larger if fragmentation happens. That's the exact problem the
old sub-address_space design for SWAP was created to solve. We only
eliminated that complexity a few months ago, and this approach seems
like it would have to bring a similar structure back to reduce
contention.

And for swapoff support: minor anonymous faults during busy periods
are indeed critical for some workloads, and being able to swapoff
cleanly is still very useful both for performance and troubleshooting.
You will need to touch many things to solve a minor fault.

For reference, I've been exploring an approach that keeps the virtual
layer runtime-optional, which avoids these overheads for workloads
that don't need virtualization:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260220-swap-table-p4-v1-0-104795d19815@tencent.com/

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2, but did not provide a specific plan for addressing this issue.

Author responded to Kairui Song's feedback about vswap performance issues in 'lots of zero-filled page' scenarios, explaining that memhog was populating memory with 0xff, triggering full swapfile-backed swap entry overhead, and suggesting a fix by changing the VSWAP_ZERO swap entry type.

Author acknowledges that MGLRU testing is relevant and offers to try it, but does not address the specific technical concern about swap backend characteristics or variance.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Kairui! Thanks a lot for the testing big boss :) I will focus on
the regression in this patch series - we can talk more about
directions in another thread :)

---

Interesting. Normally "lots of zero-filled page" is a very beneficial
case for vswap. You don't need a swapfile, or any zram/zswap metadata
overhead - it's a native swap backend. If production workload has this
many zero-filled pages, I think the numbers of vswap would be much
less alarming - perhaps even matching memory overhead because you
don't need to maintain a zram entry metadata (it's at least 2 words
per zram entry right?), while there's no reverse map overhead induced
(so it's 24 bytes on both side), and no need to do zram-side locking
:)

So I was surprised to see that it's not working out very well here. I
checked the implementation of memhog - let me know if this is wrong
place to look:

https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man8/memhog.8.html
https://github.com/numactl/numactl/blob/master/memhog.c#L52

I think this is what happened here: memhog was populating the memory
0xff, which triggers the full overhead of a swapfile-backed swap entry
because even though it's "same-filled" it's not zero-filled! I was
following Usama's observation - "less than 1% of the same-filled pages
were non-zero" - and so I only handled the zero-filled case here:

https://lore.kernel.org/all/20240530102126.357438-1-usamaarif642@gmail.com/

This sounds a bit artificial IMHO - as Usama pointed out above, I
think most samefilled pages are zero pages, in real production
workloads. However, if you think there are real use cases with a lot
of non-zero samefilled pages, please let me know I can fix this real
quick. We can support this in vswap with zero extra metadata overhead
- change the VSWAP_ZERO swap entry type to VSWAP_SAME_FILLED, then use
the backend field to store that value. I can send you a patch if
you're interested.

---

Ohh interesting - I see that you're testing with MGLRU. I can give that a try.

I'm not enabling THP/mTHP, but I don't see that you're enabling it
either - there's some 2MB swpout but that seems incidental.

Another difference is the swap backend:

1. Regarding pmem backend - I'm not sure if I can get my hands on one
of these, but if you think SSD has the same characteristics maybe I
can give that a try? The problem with SSD is for some reason variance
tends to be pretty high, between iterations yes, but especially across
reboots. Or maybe zram?

2. What about the other numbers below? Are they also on pmem? FTR I
was running most of my benchmarks on zswap, except for one kernel
build benchmark on SSD.

3. Any other backends and setup you're interested in?

BTW, sounds like you have a great benchmark suite - is it open source
somewhere? If not, can you share it with us :) Vswap aside, I think
this would be a good suite to run all swap related changes for every
swap contributor.

Once again, thank you so much for your engagement, Kairui. Very much
appreciated - I owe you a beverage of your choice whenever we meet.
And have a great rest of your day :)

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that certain workloads may initialize their heap with a fixed value, which could lead to identical swap slots and potentially cause issues in virtual swap space

Reviewer Kairui Song noted that the patch causes slower performance and broken cgroup and global swap usage accounting for zero page swaps due to skipped allocation, which is a major issue because users rely on this information.

Reviewer Kairui Song noted that the patch should not ignore the increasing speed of storage and high-speed networks, which can affect swap performance.

Reviewer Kairui Song requested that the original patch series be made publicly available, specifically mentioning a wrapper to use systemd for reboot and auto test, but noted that all relevant test steps are already posted online.
review_comments.raw_body:
I vaguely remember some workloads like Java or some JS engine
initialize their heap with fixed value, same fill might not be that
common but not a rare thing, it strongly depends on the workload.

---

Actually I don't think that's the main problem. For example, I just
wrote a few lines C bench program to zerofill ~50G of memory
and swapout sequentially:

Before:
Swapout: 4415467us
Swapin: 49573297us

After:
Swapout: 4955874us
Swapin: 56223658us

And vmstat:
cat /proc/vmstat | grep zero
thp_zero_page_alloc 0
thp_zero_page_alloc_failed 0
swpin_zero 12239329
swpout_zero 21516634

There are all zero filled pages, but still slower. And what's more, a
more critical issue, I just found the cgroup and global swap usage
accounting are both somehow broken for zero page swap,
maybe because you skipped some allocation? Users can
no longer see how many pages are swapped out. I don't think you can
break that, that's one major reason why we use a zero entry instead of
mapping to a zero readonly page. If that is acceptable, we can have
a very nice optimization right away with current swap.

That's still just an example. bypassing the accounting and still
slower is not a good sign. We should focus on the generic
performance and design.

Yet this is just another new found issue, there are many other parts
like the folio swap allocation may still occur even if a lower device
can no longer accept more whole folios, which I'm currently
unsure how it will affect swap.

---

Yeah, ZRAM has a very similar number for some cases, but storage is
getting faster and faster and swap occurs through high speed networks
too. We definitely shouldn't ignore that.

---

I can try to post that somewhere, really nothing fancy just some
wrapper to make use of systemd for reboot and auto test. But all test
steps I mentioned before are already posted and publically available.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that the swap_refs atomic type is not initialized to zero and agrees that a fix is needed.

The author acknowledged Kairui Song's concern about swap charging for non-swapfile backends, explained that the intention was to only charge towards swap usage on memory, and stated that adding new counters would be unnecessary.

Author acknowledges that simulating virtual swap space using tmpfs as a swap backend is possible and might be useful for certain benchmarks, but notes it may not work for all use cases.
review_comments.raw_body:
To a non-zero value? ISTR it was initialized to zero, but if I was
wrong then yeah it should just be a small simple patch.

---

No, that was intentional :) I probably should have documented this
better - but we're only charging towards swap usage (cgroup and system
wide) on memory. There was a whole patch that did that in the series
:)

I can add new counters to differentiate these cases, but it makes no
sense to me to charge towards swap usage for non-swapfile backend
(namely, zswap and zero swap pages). You are not actually occupying
the limited swapfile slots, but instead occupy a dynamic, vast virtual
swap space only (and memory in the case of zswap - this is actually an
argument against zram which does not do any cgroup accounting, but
that's another story for another day). I don't see a point in swap
charging here. It's the whole point of decoupling the backends - these
are not the same resource domains.

And if you follow Usama's work above, we actually were trying to
figure out a way to map it to a zero readonly page. That was Usama's
v2 of the patch series IIRC - but there was a bug. I think it was a
potential race between the reclaimer's rmap walk to unmap the page
from PTEs pointing to the page, and concurrent modifiers to the page?
We couldn't fix the race in a way that does not induce more overhead
than it's worth. But had that work we would also not do any swap
charging :)

BTW, if you can figure that part out, please let us know. We actually
quite like that idea - we just never managed to make it work (and we
have a bunch more urgent tasks).

---

I can also simulate it using tmpfs as a swap backend (although it
might not work for certain benchmarks, like your usemem benchmark in
which we allocate more memory than the host physical memory).

This is supported in zram. The support was added here:
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20251201094754.4149975-1-senozhatsky@chromium.org/ .
It is already in mainline.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledges that the current implementation has limitations, specifically for zram, where it only saves CPU work but not other potential benefits of compressed writeback. They propose two alternatives: a small-slot allocator or writing back a full backing page, which have different performance tradeoffs and are considered future work.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm aware of that work. It's an improvement, but my understanding is:

1. It only works for zram.

2. We still occupy the full PAGE_SIZE slot.

3. The writeback IO request is still of size PAGE_SIZE.

So we're saving the CPU work for decompression, but not the rest of
the potential benefits of compressed writeback.

For zswap, decoupling zswap and disk swap is a pre-requisite
(otherwise every zswap slot occupy a PAGE_SIZE slot in the swapfile
anyway).

Then, we have two alternatives. Either we implement a small-slot
allocator for swapfile-infra, or we writeback a full backing page for
compressed memory. The second option is a bit more straightforward,
but then we lose relative age of these objects - a backing page might
combine very recent compressed pages and very old compressed pages.

These approaches have different performance tradeoffs and need to be
evaluated. But anyway this is future work.

review_comments.author: Askar Safin
review_comments.summary: Reviewer asked for checksummed swap to protect against disk bit-rot and requested protection of hibernation images, noting that using dm-integrity is incompatible with mainline kernel hibernation
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you for answer!

Also, is it possible to have checksummed swap?

I want to have checksummed swap to be protected from disk bit-rot
(I already have ECC memory, so RAM is protected).

And hibernation image should be protected, too.

I tried to put swap on top of dm-integrity, but this is
incompatible with hibernation in mainline kernel.

-- 
Askar Safin

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 01/21] mm/swap: decouple swap cache from physical swap infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the swap cache from physical swap infrastructure, preparing for virtualization of swap space. It removes swap cache related helpers from the swap table and moves to a single global lock for synchronization. The swap cache will be re-partitioned into virtual swap clusters once it is moved to the virtual swap layer. This change is temporary and will be repurposed later as an rmap (physical -> virtual swap mapping).

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 02/21] swap: rearrange the swap header file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch rearranges the swap header file (include/linux/swap.h) to group related functions into categories such as lifecycle swap, swap cache, and physical swap allocator/swap device APIs. The goal is a cleaner organization of the code without introducing any functional changes. Extern keywords are removed from the functions that were rearranged.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 03/21] mm: swap: add an abstract API for locking out swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an abstract API to lock out swapoff in the Linux kernel's memory management subsystem. The current implementation of locking out swapoff is specific to the backing swap device and does not work well with the new virtual swap design, where a swap entry may not have a backing swap device. To address this issue, the patch adds two new functions: `tryget_swap_entry` and `put_swap_entry`, which provide a generic way to lock out swapoff for any swap entry. The patch also updates several kernel files to use these new functions instead of the old implementation. This change prepares the kernel for the virtual swap design and improves its flexibility in handling different types of swap entries.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 04/21] zswap: add new helpers for zswap entry operations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new helper functions to abstract away zswap entry operations, making it easier to re-implement these functions when swap is virtualized. The helpers include storing, loading, erasing, and checking if a zswap entry is empty. These functions use the xarray data structure to manage the zswap entries. The patch modifies several existing functions in the mm/zswap.c file to use these new helpers, reducing code duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 05/21] mm/swap: add a new function to check if a swap entry is in swap cached.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called `is_swap_cached` to the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) module. The function checks if a given swap entry is cached in the system's swap cache. This is done by directly accessing the swap map of the corresponding swap information structure, which minimizes overhead and ensures accurate results. The patch also updates several other functions to use this new function instead of directly checking the swap map. The motivation behind this change is to abstract away the check for swap cached state, as it will soon be managed at the virtual swap layer.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 06/21] mm: swap: add a separate type for physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new type, swp_slot_t, to represent physical swap slots on swap devices. It separates the logical view of swap entries from their physical backing state, allowing for future virtualization and dynamic allocation of swap slots. The mapping between the two types is currently an identity mapping, but will be used in later patches to associate swapped-out pages with a backing store.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 07/21] mm: create scaffolds for the new virtual swap implementation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds scaffolding code for the new virtual swap implementation, including a new mm/vswap.c source file and necessary changes to the Makefile and other related files. The vswap debugfs directory is set up, but no behavioral changes are intended at this time. Additionally, Johannes Weiner is added as a reviewer for the virtual swap development.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 08/21] zswap: prepare zswap for swap virtualization
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the zswap code for swap virtualization by removing the range partition logic and replacing it with a simple unified zswap tree. The changes include updating the zswap.h header file to remove unused functions, modifying the swapfile.c file to remove calls to these functions, and updating the zswap.c file to use the new unified zswap tree. This patch is part of a larger series that aims to virtualize swap space in Linux.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 09/21] mm: swap: allocate a virtual swap slot for each swapped out page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new virtual swap slot design, where each swapped out page is dynamically allocated a virtual slot and associated metadata structure. The virtual slot replaces the physical swap slot in page table entries and is used to index into swap data structures. The patch also repurposes the swap table infrastructure as a reverse map to look up the virtual swap slot from its associated physical swap slot on the swapfile. This change improves the management of swapped out pages and enables future optimizations such as simplified swapoff.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 10/21] swap: move swap cache to virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the swap cache and workingset shadow for anonymous pages to the virtual swap descriptor, effectively range-partitioning the swap cache by virtual swap clusters of PMD size. This change eliminates lock contention in the swap cache, improving performance. The patch updates several kernel files, including huge_memory.c, migrate.c, shmem.c, and vmscan.c, to use the new virtual swap descriptor instead of the global swap cache lock. The approach taken is to introduce a per-folio swap pointer that allows for more efficient locking and reduces contention in the swap cache.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 11/21] zswap: move zswap entry management to the virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves zswap entry management from the zswap tree to the virtual swap descriptor, eliminating lock contention between zswap entries. The change re-partitions the zswap pool by virtual swap cluster and introduces new functions for storing, loading, and erasing zswap entries in the swap descriptor. This approach simplifies the management of zswap entries and improves performance by reducing lock contention.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 12/21] swap: implement the swap_cgroup API using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the swap_cgroup API to use virtual swap, allowing for dynamic allocation of memory for swap entries' cgroup information. This reduces memory overhead in large but sparsely used swap spaces by only allocating memory when needed. The change involves moving the swap entry's cgroup information from a static array to the swap descriptor and updating various functions that interact with the swap_cgroup API.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 13/21] swap: manage swap entry lifecycle at the virtual swap layer
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the management of swap entry lifecycle to the virtual swap layer, adding two fields to the swap descriptor: in_swapcache and swap count. It re-implements all swap entry lifecycle API functions in the virtual swap layer, simplifying the logic by not implementing swap count continuation for now.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 14/21] mm: swap: decouple virtual swap slot from backing store
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the virtual swap slot from its backing store, allowing a swap entry to be backed by one of four options: physical swap slot, zero swap page, compressed object in zswap pool, or in-memory page. This change enables the use of zswap and zero swap page optimization without reserving a swapfile slot, resulting in significant disk savings for hosts with high memory usage. The patch also introduces new requirements for batched swap operations to ensure consistency across entries with different backing states.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 15/21] zswap: do not start zswap shrinker if there is no physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the zswap shrinker from starting if there are no physical swap slots available, which is a consideration for virtualized swap environments where pre-allocated swap slots do not exist.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 16/21] swap: do not unnecesarily pin readahead swap entries
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes swap readahead by not unnecessarily pinning entries in the same virtual swap cluster as the target entry, which is already pinned by the caller. The patch introduces a new function `vswap_same_cluster` to check if two virtual swap entries belong to the same vswap cluster. It then uses this function to skip unnecessary pinning of readahead entries that are already protected by the target entry's cluster. This change aims to improve performance and reduce overhead in swap operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 17/21] swapfile: remove zeromap bitmap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the zeromap bitmap from the swapfile code in the Linux kernel. The zeromap bitmap was used to track zero pages, but since zero swap entries are now treated as a separate backend in the virtual swap layer, this bitmap is no longer needed. Removing it saves memory overhead by eliminating 1 bit per swap page. This change does not affect behavior and only optimizes memory usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 18/21] memcg: swap: only charge physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory cgroup (memcg) swap handling to only charge physical swap slots, rather than virtual swap slots. This change is made possible by the removal of zswap and zero-filled swap page optimization from consuming physical swap space. The patch updates various functions in the memcontrol code to record and clear swap charges for memcgs, ensuring that only actual physical swap usage is accounted for.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 19/21] swap: simplify swapoff using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies and optimizes the swapoff process by utilizing virtual swap slots stored at page table entries, eliminating the need for a page table walk. Instead of traversing all VMAs, it iterates through allocated swap slots on the swapfile, finds their corresponding virtual swap slots, and faults them in. This results in cleaner code and a performance improvement of about 13% kernel CPU time reduction compared to the original baseline.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 20/21] swapfile: replace the swap map with bitmaps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the swap map in the Linux kernel's swapfile code with two bitmaps, one for tracking allocated swap entries and another for bad swap entries. This change is made possible by moving the swap count state to the virtual swap layer, which reduces the number of states that need to be tracked from 4 to 3. The use of bitmaps instead of a single array saves 6 bits per swap entry, reducing memory usage. The patch updates the swap_info_struct structure in include/linux/swap.h and modifies several functions in mm/swapfile.c to work with the new bitmap-based representation.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the vswap_free function by batching contiguous free calls to reduce overhead on non-disk-swap backends. It achieves this by introducing a new release_vswap_slot_nr function that takes an additional 'nr' parameter, which specifies the number of slots to be released. This allows for more efficient handling of consecutive free operations and reduces unnecessary lock drops.

Suggested exploring alternative approaches such as a small-slot allocator or writing back a full backing page.
